verbatim: For he has made plain to us through all the Prophets that he needs neither sacrifices nor burnt-offerings nor oblations, saying in one place, What is the multitude of your sacrifices unto me? saith the Lord. I am full of burnt offerings and desire not the fat of lambs and the blood of bulls and goats, not even when ye come to appear before me. For who has required these things at your hands? Henceforth shall ye tread my court no more. If ye bring flour, it is vain. Incense is an abomination to me. I cannot away with your new moons and sabbaths. These things then he abolished in order that the new law of our Lord Jesus Christ, which is without the yoke of necessity, might have its oblation not made by man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author argues that God has formally abolished the Jewish system of material sacrifices and offerings in favor of a new law, thereby removing the legal/religious validity of those traditional Jewish practices.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to claim the divine covenant
verbatim: take heed to yourselves now, and be not made like unto some, heaping up your sins and saying that the covenant is both theirs and ours. It is ours: but in this way did they finally lose it when Moses had just received it, for the Scripture says: And Moses was in the mount fasting forty days and forty nights, and he received the covenant from the Lord, tables of stone written with the finger of the hand of the Lord. But they turned to idols and lost it. For thus saith the Lord: Moses, Moses, go down quickly, for thy people, whom thou broughtest forth out of the land of Egypt, have broken the Law. And Moses understood and cast the two tables out of his hands, and their covenant was broken, in order that the covenant of Jesus the Beloved should be sealed in our hearts in hope of his faith.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author explicitly denies that the covenant belongs to the Jews, or to both Jews and Christians; he asserts that the Jews lost the right to the covenant through idolatry at Sinai, transferring that status exclusively to the new community (Christians).

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from the yoke of necessity
verbatim: These things then he abolished in order that the new law of our Lord Jesus Christ, which is without the yoke of necessity, might have its oblation not made by man.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage defines the new group (followers of Christ) as being governed by a law characterized by the lack of 'the yoke of necessity' typical of the old Jewish ritual law.

verbatim: But to us he says, Behold this is the fast which I chose, saith the Lord, loose every bond of wickedness, set loose the fastenings of harsh agreements, send away the bruised in forgiveness, and tear up every unjust contract, give to the hungry thy bread, and if thou seest a naked man clothe him, bring the homeless into thy house... Then thou shalt cry and God shall hear thee; while thou art still speaking He shall say, ‘Lo I am here’
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author outlines the specific rules of the new community's 'fast'—based on social justice and mercy—as the condition for receiving the resource of God answering their prayers.

verbatim: The commandment was written, Whosoever does not keep the fast shall die the death, and the Lord commanded this because he himself was going to offer the vessel of the spirit as a sacrifice... Attend carefully,—and let all the priests alone eat the entrails unwashed with vinegar. Why? Because you are going to give to me gall and vinegar to drink when I am on the point of offering my flesh for my new people, therefore you alone shall eat, while the people fast and mourn in sackcloth and ashes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes a specific religious regulation where the social group of priests is granted the exclusive right to consume specific parts of the sacrifice (entrails) during a fast, while the general population is prohibited from eating and must mourn.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to validity of physical circumcision
verbatim: But moreover the circumcision in which they trusted has been abolished. For he declared that circumcision was not of the flesh, but they erred because an evil angel was misleading them... Behold, saith the Lord, all the heathen are uncircumcised in the foreskin, but this people is uncircumcised in heart.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author explicitly states that the physical mark of circumcision, which defined the Jewish group's covenantal status, has been 'abolished' as a valid ritual requirement, reassigned instead to a spiritual/metaphorical 'circumcision of the heart'.

verbatim: the commandment has been given to Israel that the men in whom sin is complete offer a heifer and slay it... and that boys then take the ashes and put them into vessels and bind scarlet wool on sticks... and that the boys all sprinkle the people thus one by one in order that they all be purified from their sins?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a ritual rule where 'boys' (defined by age) are the specific group tasked with the resource/authority of performing the purification sprinkling on the community, whereas 'sinful men' are restricted to the slaughtering/burning.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the divine covenant
verbatim: Now let us see whether this people or the former people is the heir, and whether the covenant is for us or for them.

Moses received the two tables, written by the finger of the hand of the Lord in the Spirit ; and Moses took them, and carried them down to give them to the people. And the Lord said to Moses... thy people whom thou didst bring out of the land of Egypt have broken the Law. And Moses perceived that they had made themselves again molten images, and he cast them out of his hands, and the tables of the covenant of the Lord were broken. Moses received it, but they were not worthy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author argues that while the Jews (the 'former people') were intended to receive the covenant via Moses, their sin with the molten images resulted in the physical breaking of the tables, symbolizing their loss of the right to that resource. The text explicitly asks who the 'heir' is and concludes the Jews were not worthy.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit the divine covenant
verbatim: But learn how we received it. Moses received it when he was a servant, but the Lord himself gave it to us, as the people of the inheritance, by suffering for our sakes.

And it was made manifest both that the tale of their sins should be completed in their sins, and that we through Jesus, the Lord who inherits the covenant, should receive it, for he was prepared for this purpose, that when he appeared he might redeem from darkness our hearts... and by his word might make a covenant with us.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies his own group ('we', the Christians) as the legitimate heirs of the covenant previously lost by the Jews, granted through the suffering and mediation of Jesus.

verbatim: Concerning the water it has been written with regard to Israel that they will not receive the baptism that brings the remission of sins, but will build for themselves.
He means to say that we go down into the water full of sins and foulness, and we come up bearing the fruit of fear in our hearts, and having hope on Jesus in the Spirit.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule of religious practice where the group (believers/Christians) gains the specific resource of 'remission of sins' and spiritual 'fruit' specifically through the act of descending into the water (baptism).

verbatim: Concerning the water it has been written with regard to Israel that they will not receive the baptism that brings the remission of sins, but will build for themselves.
For the Prophet says... this people hath committed two evils: they have deserted me, the spring of life, and they have dug for themselves a cistern of death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author contrasts the Christian access to baptism with 'Israel' (the Jews), stating explicitly that they will not receive the baptism that brings remission of sins, effectively barred from this spiritual resource by their rejection of the 'spring of life'.

verbatim: Thou shalt not withhold thy hand from thy son or from thy daughter, but shalt teach them the fear of God from their youth up.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author is instructing the early Christian community on the 'Way of Light,' establishing a rule that parents must provide religious education (the fear of God) to their children from a young age.

verbatim: thou shalt not command in bitterness thy slave or handmaid who hope on the same God, lest they cease to fear the God who is over you both; for he came not to call men with respect of persons, but those whom the Spirit prepared.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly prohibits masters from commanding their slaves with bitterness. This is framed as a general rule of conduct within the community to ensure slaves continue to fear God rather than just their earthly masters.

verbatim: thou shalt not command in bitterness thy slave or handmaid who hope on the same God, lest they cease to fear the God who is over you both; for he came not to call men with respect of persons, but those whom the Spirit prepared.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage establishes that slaves and masters 'hope on the same God' and that God calls people without 'respect of persons,' granting slaves the same spiritual status and right to divine calling as free persons.

verbatim: Thou shalt obey thy masters as a type of God in modesty and fear; thou shalt not command in bitterness thy slave or handmaid who hope on the same God, lest they cease to fear the God who is over you both
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mandates that those in a position of servitude must obey their masters, framing the master as a 'type of God,' which reinforces the social hierarchy through religious obligation.

verbatim: Thou shalt share all things with thy neighbour and shall not say that they are thy own property; for if you are sharers in that which is incorruptible, how much more in that which is corruptible?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The rule mandates a system of communal sharing where individuals are forbidden from claiming exclusive ownership of material goods, ensuring the poor have access to the resources of their neighbors.

verbatim: knowing not the reward of righteousness, who cleave not to the good, nor to righteous judgment, who attend not to the cause of the widow and orphan... advocates of the rich, unjust judges of the poor, altogether sinful.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes the 'Way of Darkness' by listing the systemic failures of the current society, specifically identifying that judges are unjust toward the poor and favor the rich, effectively depriving the poor of fair legal protection.

verbatim: knowing not the reward of righteousness, who cleave not to the good, nor to righteous judgment, who attend not to the cause of the widow and orphan, spending wakeful nights not in the fear of God, but in the pursuit of vice
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the neglect of 'the cause of the widow and orphan' as a characteristic of the 'Way of Darkness,' indicating that these vulnerable groups are systematically denied legal attention or protection in the described society.
